title: Gold hits all-time high above $2,800 as central bank buying accelerates and geopolitical risks mount
topicContext: XAU/USD tracks the price of gold in U.S. dollars. Gold is traditionally a safe-haven asset that rises during periods of geopolitical uncertainty, falling real interest rates, and dollar weakness. Central bank gold purchases have accelerated since 2022 as several countries seek to diversify reserves away from dollar-denominated assets. The current rally has been driven by a combination of central bank buying (particularly China, India, and Turkey), geopolitical tensions in the Middle East, and expectations that the Fed will eventually need to cut rates. Gold is also sensitive to inflation expectations — it tends to outperform when investors fear that central banks are behind the curve on inflation.
body:
Gold surged to an all-time high of $2,812 per ounce on Tuesday, extending a rally that has seen the precious metal gain 18% since the start of the year as investors seek safety amid escalating geopolitical tensions and persistent concerns about the global inflation outlook.

The latest leg higher was triggered by data showing that central banks collectively purchased 387 tonnes of gold in the first quarter of 2026, the highest quarterly total on record. China's central bank was the largest buyer, adding 62 tonnes to its reserves, followed by India and Poland. Turkey, which had been a net seller in late 2025, reversed course and added 28 tonnes.

Analysts said the central bank buying reflects a structural shift in global reserve management. 'This isn't tactical — it's strategic,' said the head of commodities research at a major European bank. 'Central banks are telling you that they want less dollar exposure and more hard assets. That's a multi-year trend that supports gold prices well above $2,500.'

The rally has also been fueled by retail and institutional investor demand. Gold ETF holdings have risen for 14 consecutive weeks, the longest streak of inflows since 2020. Open interest in COMEX gold futures hit a record last week.

The strength in gold is creating a dilemma for the Federal Reserve. Higher gold prices are both a symptom and a cause of inflation expectations becoming less anchored. If investors are buying gold because they don't trust central banks to control inflation, that belief can become self-fulfilling.

Silver rose in sympathy, gaining 3.2% to $34.80 per ounce. Platinum and palladium also advanced. Mining stocks outperformed broader equity indices, with the NYSE Arca Gold Miners Index up 5.1%.

Some analysts cautioned that gold is now trading at historically stretched valuations relative to real interest rates and could be vulnerable to a sharp correction if the Fed delivers a hawkish surprise. 'Gold is priced for perfection,' warned one strategist. 'Any sign that the Fed is serious about higher-for-longer, and you could see a fast $100 pullback.'
